summary: Well-structured product-ideal blueprint that clearly delineates runtime deploy scope from tunnel/controller ownership. Strong self-description and local recoverability — a reader can fully reconstruct the deploy contract from this file plus the language root. Main weaknesses are semantic redundancy between enforces/defines/when-then blocks and a stray comment line that violates Agentish.rejects('Comments'). Operational specificity (CDK ordering, named tunnel mode, AWS fetch paths) suggests active use but no direct cross-reference evidence confirms it.
strengths: Role header immediately communicates the file's exact scope: operator rollout path that must not interfere with tunnel ownership
Enforces block encodes detailed, non-obvious operational constraints (CDK ordering, token secret preparation, hostname metadata fetch) that preload agent reasoning about deploy boundaries
Defines block maps every policy name to a concrete operational meaning, keeping interpretive slack low
Tunnel ownership boundary is stated from multiple angles (deploy side, controller side, named-tunnel mode), reducing ambiguity for agents encountering partial context
Abstraction stays at product-ideal level throughout — no implementation commands or script paths leak in
weaknesses: When/then blocks largely restate constraints already covered in the enforces block, reducing net semantic density without adding new causal or conditional information
Line 3 contains a plain comment ('// Deploy Manager Runtime') which Agentish explicitly rejects

Enforces block mixes runtime-deploy rules with pre-deploy infrastructure-provisioning concerns (tunnel provisioning, DNS routes, token secrets before CDK), slightly blurring the file's stated scope of 'runtime deploy'
No companion files were provided that reference this blueprint, so live-use evidence relies entirely on internal specificity rather than confirmed cross-references
